title: 1.8 System Maintenance, SOPs, & Troubleshooting
lecture:
1. Conceptual Breakdown (The "What" and "Why")

When running a production automation engine, code bugs and server outages are inevitable. If a server hosting your application goes offline or crashes, or if an external service like OpenAI or Anthropic is temporarily down, how do we prevent lead loss?

First, we containerize our code using Docker. Instead of relying on a local computer's specific node settings, Docker wraps the entire app in a lightweight virtual container. To make these containers secure and small, we use a Multi-Stage Build Pattern:

  • Stage 1 (Builder): Installs dev dependencies, TypeScript compilers, and compiles our code to standard JavaScript in a `/dist` directory. This environment contains a lot of bloated compiler files.
  • Stage 2 (Production Runner): Starts with a fresh Node.js Alpine base image, installs only the lean runtime dependencies, and copies over the compiled `/dist` JavaScript. This reduces the container size from 1GB to about 150MB, speeding up deployment and reducing security vulnerability surfaces.

Second, we handle API failures inside our message queues using **Ack/Nack Logic**:**

  • Acknowledge (Ack): Tells the Pub/Sub queue: 'We successfully processed this lead. You can safely delete it from the queue.'
  • Negative Acknowledge (Nack): Tells the Pub/Sub queue: 'An error occurred (e.g., rate limits hit or API down). Do not delete this lead. Keep it safe in the queue and try sending it to us again in a few minutes.'

2. Code-Level & File Walkthrough

The container and background queue configurations are found in:

  • [Dockerfile](file:///Users/ashleybutler/ai-funnel-machine/Dockerfile): Configures the multi-stage build.
  • [subscriber.ts](file:///Users/ashleybutler/ai-funnel-machine/src/subscriber.ts): Manages Pub/Sub event processing and acknowledgements.

Let's look at the subscriber loop in subscriber.ts:

subscription.on('message', async (message: Message) => {
  const lead = parseLead(message);
  if (!lead) {
    message.ack(); // Bad format - delete so it doesn't block the queue
    return;
  }

  try {
    await handler(lead); // Run agents & calendar bookings
    message.ack(); // Success - delete lead
  } catch (err) {
    console.error('Handler failed, nacking message', err);
    message.nack(); // Failure - retry processing later
  }
});

Line-by-Line Breakdown:

  • Lines 2-6: If a message has corrupt JSON and cannot be parsed into a valid lead structure, we call message.ack() to remove it. If we nacked it, it would loop forever, clogging the queue (this is called a poison pill).
  • Lines 8-10: We execute our main agentic qualification and booking pipeline. If it executes without error, we call message.ack().
  • Lines 11-14: If an API call fails or times out, the catch block catches the error, logs it, and calls message.nack(). This tells Google Cloud to hold the message and send it back to the server for a retry after a backoff duration.
exercise:
3. Step-by-Step Guided Exercise

Examine the Docker multi-stage configuration and simulate a background task restart to verify logs work.

  • Step A: Open the file: /Users/ashleybutler/ai-funnel-machine/Dockerfile in your editor.
  • Step B: Locate the AS builder segment on line 1, and the second FROM node:22-alpine statement on line 9. Notice how no source files (.ts) are copied into the final production image.
  • Step C: Check that your dev server is running by inspecting the terminal output of the active background dev task (which runs npm run dev).
  • Step D: If you want to check logs of the running subscriber, open a terminal window and check the logs using terminal utilities. You should see Subscribed to leads-sub in the log history, verifying connection.

quizzes.question: What is the primary benefit of using a Multi-Stage Dockerfile build?
options: It speeds up the SQLite SELECT query performance.
It compiles TypeScript files directly into SQL databases.
It minimizes the final production image size by leaving compilers and devDependencies out of the final runner image.
It translates Python code to JavaScript.
quizzes.explanation: Multi-stage builds allow you to run heavy compile processes in one container, and then copy only the static compiled output files to a fresh, small container for deployment, keeping the image small and secure.

quizzes.question: What happens if a background worker throws an error during lead qualification and calls message.nack()?
options: The lead record is permanently deleted from the queue.
The message is returned to the queue and will be retried automatically by the subscriber later.
The database is dropped.
The developer's Slack account is deleted.
quizzes.explanation: Nacking (Negative Acknowledging) a message tells the queue broker that processing failed. The broker puts the message back in the queue to be delivered again, ensuring reliability.

quizzes.question: If an incoming message contains corrupt JSON that cannot be parsed, why must we call message.ack() instead of message.nack()?
options: To save space on Google Drive.
To prevent a 'poison pill' loop, where a permanently unparseable message continuously loops and clogs the queue processing.
To force the server to restart.
To write the corrupt message to the SQLite database.
quizzes.explanation: If a message is corrupt, retrying it will fail forever. We must acknowledge it (ack) to remove it from the active queue, avoiding a poison pill blockage.

quizzes.question: Which Alpine-based node image version is used to build and run the containers in the project Dockerfile?
options: node:16-slim
node:22-alpine
node:latest-ubuntu
node:18-debian
quizzes.explanation: The Dockerfile specifies node:22-alpine for both the builder and runtime stages, using the lightweight alpine-linux distribution.

quizzes.question: In which file is the Pub/Sub subscriber listener loop configured?
options: src/server.ts
src/subscriber.ts
src/lead.ts
src/config.ts
quizzes.explanation: src/subscriber.ts initiates the Pub/Sub subscription event listener and handles message dispatching, acknowledgements, and error retries.
